architecture_fit.note: Plain scaled-dot-product attention mis-scaled by sqrt(d) on unit-norm vectors squashes logits; result barely beats chance (auc 0.014 vs 0.0125).

baseline_comparison.note: Chance (1/K) line is plotted, but method sits essentially on top of it, so there is no 'works vs fails' contrast to show.

faithfulness.note: README narrates a hand-built sparse routing/prototype/expansion circuit that does not exist in main.py — code is vanilla softmax attention; no ablation.

operating_range.note: Sweep spans rho 0.25–4.0 (K=16–256) but the method collapses to chance everywhere — silent failure across the whole range.

hardcoded_weights_bonus.note: N/A — the claimed hardcoded circuit is not actually implemented; code is standard attention, so nothing to credit.

visual_judgement.note: Plot design is clean (curve + error bars + chance + AUC), but find_latest_results filters for 'results_'-prefixed dirs that never exist, so the Demo renders 'No results found'.

visualisation_rationale.note: Rationale correctly motivates target-attention-vs-rho with the 1/K baseline, though it labels the curve 'Standard Attention', contradicting the README's circuit claim.

notes: The README describes an elaborate hand-built sparse causal circuit, but main.py implements plain scaled-dot-product attention scaled by sqrt(d), which on unit-norm queries/keys squashes the logits to near-uniform — target attention barely exceeds the 1/K chance baseline at every rho (lift_over_linear_auc = 0.0015). The documented mechanism is fabricated relative to the code, there is no causal/ablation evidence, and the Gradio Demo can't even load results because it filters for 'results_'-prefixed run dirs that don't exist.
